abstract: Bacterial wound infections remain a global health crisis due to the rapid rise of multidrug-resistant pathogens. In this study, we developed an innovative, antibiotic-free antimicrobial strategy using bimetallic hybrid nanozymes. These nanozymes (FMA NPs) were engineered by growing ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) in situ onto a metal-organic framework (MOF) shell that stabilizes a magnetite (Fe3O4) core. The resulting hybrid structure exhibits powerful synergistic peroxidase (POD)-like activity, enabling the generation of high levels of toxic hydroxyl radicals (·OH) even at physiological concentrations of H2O2 (0.97 mM). Our results demonstrate that FMA NPs possess excellent biocompatibility and exert potent antibacterial effects against both Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria, effectively disrupting established biofilms. In vivo animal models further confirmed that the hybrid nanozymes significantly accelerate wound healing with a high degree of safety and no detectable systemic toxicity. This well-designed nanocatalytic platform represents a promising antibiotic-independent therapy for managing infected wounds in clinical settings.
